comments.author: rockyj
comments.textRawHtml:
This is a total mess IMHO.

- The make around 5 billion in revenue per quarter - The problem according to them is profit margin - around 150-160 million

So first of all, they are big! Secondly they are not at a loss. They just have a "thin, non-growing margin". So to fix all this they are trimming down, so they can "return to growth" (which I think is ridiculous).

Some points -

- They are huge business even now - 5 billion per quarter revenue is no joke

- They did not have to buy all those studios

- They looked at Netflix, and wanted the sweet monthly subscription cash stream

- Then they did not have to give away popular games day one on Game Pass

- And finally, they did not have to raise Game Pass prices to improve the profit margins. Of course, consumers pulled out.

- Once again, short term vision, crazy decisions, bad spending spree and a constant need to "make numbers go up" and who has to pay for all this?
comments.textZhHtml:
恕我直言，这真是一团糟。

- 每季度收入约为 50 亿美元 - 他们认为问题是利润率 - 大约150-1.6亿

所以首先，他们很大！其次，他们并不不知所措。他们只是拥有“微薄且不增长的利润”。因此，为了解决所有这些问题，他们正在削减开支，这样他们就可以“恢复增长”（我认为这很荒谬）。

一些观点 -

- 即使现在他们也是巨大的业务 - 每季度 50 亿的收入不是开玩笑

- 他们不必购买所有这些工作室

- 他们关注 Netflix，并想要每月可观的订阅现金流

- 然后他们不必在第一天就放弃受欢迎的游戏关于 Game Pass

- 最后，他们不必提高 Game Pass 价格来提高利润率。当然，消费者退出了。

- 短期愿景、疯狂决策、糟糕的消费狂潮以及“让数字上升”的持续需求，谁必须为这一切买单？

children.author: mattalex
children.textRawHtml:
150M at 5B revenue is not great: that's 3% margin!

The bigger issue is that console manufacturer revenue is highly cyclical. This is hard to see in e.g. Xbox and Sony since they are both part of a larger conglomerate, but really obvious for nintendo.

You generally have a cycle of - "Launch": high marketing costs, low/negative HW margins - "Mid-cycle": lowering manufacturing costs, large game sales, high margin DLCs - "end-of-cycle": falling HW sales, fewer exclusives (-> preparation for next gen), fewer consumers (-> waiting for next gen). Here you usually have maximum profits since you don't subsidize HW and marketing is minimal (platforms are already locked in)

Generally you have to establish a big userbase during the mid-cycle such that you can levarage it during the late-cycle to be able to afford next-gen. Xbox has the big issue that their mid-cycle was catastrophic, which means they now don't have the console base to get into the next generation: If they have 3% margin _right now_ in the end-of-cycle where marketing and development costs are at their lowest, this does not bode well for the overall health of the business.
children.textZhHtml:
1.5 亿美元的 5B 收入并不高：只有 3% 的利润！

更大的问题是游戏机制造商的收入具有高度周期性。这在例如中很难看到。 Xbox 和索尼，因为它们都是更大企业集团的一部分，但对于任天堂来说确实很明显。

通常有一个循环 - “启动”：营销成本高，硬件利润率低/负 - “中周期”：降低制造成本、游戏销量大、DLC 高利润 - “周期结束”：硬件销量下降、独家产品减少（-> 为下一代做准备）、消费者减少（-> 等待下一代）。在这里，你通常会获得最大的利润，因为你不补贴硬件，而且营销也很少（平台已经锁定）

通常，你必须在中期建立一个庞大的用户群，这样你就可以在周期后期利用它来负担下一代。 Xbox 有一个大问题，他们的中期周期是灾难性的，这意味着他们现在没有进入下一代的控制台基础： 如果他们在营销和开发成本最低的周期结束时_现在_有 3% 的利润，这对于企业的整体健康状况来说并不是一个好兆头。

children.author: Drupon
children.textRawHtml:
>150M at 5B revenue is not great: that's 3% margin!

Oy vey maybe this kind of money grubbery is not good for our society.
children.textZhHtml:
>1.5 亿美元的 5B 收入并不好：那是 3% 的利润！

哦，也许这种肮脏的金钱对我们的社会不利。

children.author: lelanthran
children.textRawHtml:
> Oy vey maybe this kind of money grubbery is not good for our society.

No, it's a signal from the market that the product being sold is not wanted by the market.

They've mismanaged all their IP pursuing that yummy subscription revenue. Turns out gamers really don't like to buy subscriptions. As a poster downthread pointed out, the games that are not always-on and subscription-based are doing fine. It's the recent AAA model of subscription that is bleeding money.
children.textZhHtml:
> 哦，也许这种金钱肮脏的行为对我们的社会没有好处。

不，这是市场发出的信号，表明市场不想要所销售的产品。

为了追求可观的订阅收入，他们对所有 IP 进行了管理不善。事实证明，游戏玩家真的不喜欢购买订阅。正如发帖者下线指出的那样，并非始终在线且基于订阅的游戏表现良好。最近的 AAA 订阅模式正在大量流失资金。

children.author: Orygin
children.textRawHtml: Oh yeah I forgot 5 BILLION dollar in revenue is a signal that the market does not want your product
children.textZhHtml: 哦，是的，我忘了 50 亿美元的收入是市场不想要你的产品的信号

children.author: lelanthran
children.textRawHtml:
> Oh yeah I forgot 5 BILLION dollar in revenue is a signal that the market does not want your product

Doesn't matter how big the revenue is if the market is not interested at the price you are selling.

I mean, by your logic, if I sell a dollar for 64c, and do $5b revenue, that's an indication that the market does indeed want the product, but not an indication that the market wants the product at the price you need to sell at to stay in business.
children.textZhHtml:
> 哦，是的，我忘记了 50 亿美元的收入是市场不想要你的产品的信号

如果市场对你销售的价格不感兴趣，收入有多大并不重要。

我的意思是，按照你的逻辑，如果我以 64c 的价格出售 1 美元，并获得 50 亿美元的收入，这表明市场确实需要该产品，但并不表明市场想要该产品以维持业务所需的销售价格。

children.author: nayroclade
children.textRawHtml: They are not selling at a loss though. The real issue here is that businesses such as Microsoft do not exist to serve a market or make enough money to survive and pay their employees. They exist to increase the value of their owner's shares. This is predicated on an unsustainable model of continual growth, with the expectation that every market they operate in can produce high margins and endless expansion. But demand is not infinite and persistent high margins are the exception, not the norm.
children.textZhHtml: 不过，他们并没有亏本出售。这里真正的问题是，像微软这样的企业的存在并不是为了服务市场或赚取足够的钱来生存和支付员工工资。它们的存在是为了增加所有者股票的价值。这是基于不可持续的持续增长模式，期望他们经营的每个市场都能产生高利润和无限扩张。但需求并不是无限的，持续的高利润率只是例外，而不是常态。

children.author: bluGill
children.textRawHtml: Maybe it isn't a loss, but as an investor (never directly in them), I consider 3% profit margin a bad sign - at that return I'd prefer a savings account: FDIC insurance means that after accounting for risks the savings account is better.  I know stock returns don't directly track profit margin, but that is one input into the complex consideration of stocks.
children.textZhHtml: 也许这不算亏损，但作为一名投资者（虽然从不直接投资它们），我认为3%的利润率是个糟糕的信号——有这种回报率我还不如存银行：联邦存款保险意味着，在计入风险因素后，储蓄账户更划算。我知道股票回报并不直接追踪利润率，但这是综合考量股票时的一个输入因素。

children.author: vouwfietsman
children.textRawHtml:
You are right, and this is why not everything should be a stock. Whatever they will cut to avoid emitting "a bad sign", may involve:

* firing people * making services worse * sacrificing their own future

Whether it actually does involve those things is effectively arbitrary, because the consideration of the "bad sign" is also arbitrary. If there is no objective value judgement of their operation there is no objective value judgement in their streamlining either, so all bets are off.

no percentage no good

"complex consideration"
children.textZhHtml:
你说得对，这也是为什么并非所有东西都该变成股票。他们为了避免释放“糟糕信号”而削减的任何东西，都可能涉及：

* 裁员 * 降低服务质量 * 牺牲自身未来

实际上是否真的涉及这些，完全是随意的，因为对“糟糕信号”的考量本身也是随意的。如果对他们的运营没有客观的价值判断，那么对他们精简业务的判断也不会有客观标准，一切就都说不准了。

没利润率就没好结果

“综合考量”

children.author: pfdietz
children.textRawHtml:
Overall they are not selling at a loss, but parts of what they're selling are being sold at a loss. The market is telling them to slim the f- down and get rid of those money-losing parts.

This is all normal and justifiable. Where is the logic that corporations need to preserve dysfunctional parts of their operations?
children.textZhHtml:
总体来看他们并没有亏本卖，但他们卖的东西里有一部分是在亏本卖。市场在告诉他们，赶紧大幅瘦身，砍掉那些亏钱的业务。

这完全正常且合理。企业必须保留其运营中那些功能失调的部分，这有什么逻辑可言？

children.author: tfigueroa
children.textRawHtml: Corporations don’t need to, no. But they are systems, and it’s a beginner mistake to assume changing one part is going to affect the whole in some simple, predictable, logical way.
children.textZhHtml: 企业确实没必要，没错。但它们是系统，认为改变某一部分就会以某种简单、可预测、符合逻辑的方式影响整体，这是初学者的错误。

children.author: pfdietz
children.textRawHtml:
That sounds like an extremely lame excuse to preserve money losing activities.

I'll counter by saying that pruning off failing things is not only good, it's the core of capitalism. Creative destruction, as Schumpeter called it. You get efficiency by hunting down and eliminating inefficiency, redeploying the resources elsewhere.
children.textZhHtml:
这听起来像是为了维持亏损业务而找的极其蹩脚的借口。

我要反驳的是，剥离失败业务不仅有益，而且是资本主义的核心。正如熊彼特所说的创造性破坏。通过揪出并消除低效环节，将资源重新配置到其他地方，你就能实现高效。

children.author: vouwfietsman
children.textRawHtml:
> pruning off failing things is not only good, it's the core of capitalism

It's quite serious that you see "being good" as something inferior to "the core of capitalism".

Also, the core of capitalism is making money for private individuals, nothing more, nothing less. Whether that's done with or without "failing things", is really beside the point.
children.textZhHtml:
> 剥离失败业务不仅有益，而且是资本主义的核心

你把“向善”看得比“资本主义的核心”更低等，这问题相当严重。

此外，资本主义的核心就是为私人牟利，仅此而已，别无其他。这到底是在有“失败业务”还是没有“失败业务”的情况下进行的，其实根本不重要。

children.author: jayers
children.textRawHtml:
The core of capitalism is about where ownership of capital (value producing assets) resides, that is, by private individuals.

What private individuals choose to do with their capital, chase infinite growth and profit or sit on it, is up to them. This is as opposed to say, state ownership of capital.

People confuse the stock market with capitalism. You don't need a stock market for capitalism to function. Publicly traded companies in the United States are legally bound to maximize profit (Dodge vs. Ford Motor Co.)
children.textZhHtml:
资本主义的核心在于资本（创造价值的资产）的所有权归属，即归私人所有。

私人个体选择如何处理其资本，是追求无限的增长和利润，还是将其闲置，都取决于他们自己。这与国家拥有资本所有权等情况截然不同。

人们常常把股票市场与资本主义混为一谈。资本主义的运行并不需要股票市场。美国的上市公司在法律上有义务实现利润最大化（道奇诉福特汽车公司案）。

children.author: xerox13ster
children.textRawHtml:
> You get efficiency by hunting down and eliminating inefficiency, redeploying the resources elsewhere

Plants do this. What’s it got to do with capitalism?
children.textZhHtml:
> 通过揪出并消除低效环节，将资源重新配置到其他地方，你就能实现高效

植物也会这么做。这跟资本主义有什么关系？

children.author: pfdietz
children.textRawHtml:
Error is rampant. I saw a quote saying the difference between a good business and bad one is the good one makes the right decision 60% of time, the bad one 40% of the time.

So errors abound, and have to be subsequently corrected. This correction process is as natural to capitalism as breathing is to you being alive. Without it, things would rapidly grind to a halt. We see this in sclerotic centrally planned economies where errors persist for much longer.
children.textZhHtml:
错误无处不在。我曾看到一句引言说，好企业和差企业的区别在于，好企业有60%的时间能做出正确决策，而差企业只有40%。

因此，错误比比皆是，且必须随后加以纠正。这种纠正过程对资本主义而言，就像呼吸对于生命一样自然。如果没有它，一切很快就会陷入停滞。我们在僵化的中央计划经济中就能看到这种情况，那里的错误会持续更长的时间。

children.author: tommit
children.textRawHtml:
No? By that logic you sell a dollar for 103c and that's not enough to quench your greed.

Obviously both statements are gross oversimplifications. But I could not help myself and let that slide just like that.
children.textZhHtml:
不是吗？照这种逻辑，你把一美元卖103美分都填不满你的贪欲。

显然这两种说法都严重过度简化了。但我实在忍不住，不能就这么放任这种说法不管。

children.author: moron4hire
children.textRawHtml: Just to add to your comment, people do sell dollars for $1.07, they are called mortgages. I've heard it's a pretty big business.
children.textZhHtml: 补充一下你的评论，人们确实会把一美元卖1.07美元，这叫抵押贷款。我听说这可是个大生意。

children.author: jstummbillig
children.textRawHtml: What they do is they sell a dollar for 1.07$ future dollars (and, realistically, less than that because of defaults but whatever).
children.textZhHtml: 他们做的是把一美元卖成未来的1.07美元（实际上，考虑到违约情况，还卖不到这么多，但无所谓了）。

children.author: tialaramex
children.textRawHtml:
It's a lot more than $1.07 future money, a mortgage is more like $2 or these days $5

If you're offering even 25 year 75% LTV mortgages at such a low interest rate it's $1.07 future money you're going bankrupt. And these days people are taking 30 year, 95% LTV or even asking for 40 year 100% LTV which is fully batshit.
children.textZhHtml:
这远不止是1.07美元的未来资金，抵押贷款更像是2美元，或者按现在的行情说是5美元。

如果你以这么低的利率提供25年期75%贷款价值比的抵押贷款，指望靠1.07美元的未来资金，那你只会破产。而现在人们都在申请30年期95%贷款价值比的贷款，甚至要求40年期100%贷款价值比的贷款，这简直疯了。

stories.title: Every new car sold in the European Union must include a driver monitoring camera
stories.titleZh: 欧盟境内销售的每一辆新车都必须配备驾驶员监测摄像头。

comments.author: dvratil
comments.textRawHtml:
I sometimes wonder how these systems are being tested on the road and whether there's any feedback from the test drivers, or what kind of morons are there saying "this is completely fine, exactly like intended" when they read the feedback...

My car has adaptive cruise control and will automatically adjust speed based on speed limit signs. I was on a highway at 130km/h and the car read a 60km/h speed limit sign that was on an exit shoulder (already separated by a concrete barrier from the highway, so technically a different road altogether) and started breaking really fast - I was pretty close from getting tailgated by the driver behind me, who did not (rightfully) expect me to suddenly start breaking with nothing in front of me. Luckily this can be permanently turned off, so I can continue using cruise control without being afraid of every single speed limit sign.

Recently I had rented a Skoda Karoq (very new one, probably 2024/2025) which adjusted the cruise control speed not even based on signs, but probably based on data from built-in maps? I don't know - but it would randomly decide that I entered a 20km/h zone while driving on a 90km/h road. And this couldn't be turned off. So I just turned off cruise control completely, because wtf, how can anyone think this is improving road safety?

Edit: typo
comments.textZhHtml:
我有时想知道这些系统是如何在道路上进行测试的，以及测试驾驶员是否有任何反馈，或者有哪些白痴在阅读反馈时说“这完全没问题，完全符合预期”...

我的车有自适应巡航控制系统，会根据限速标志自动调整速度。我在高速公路上以 130 公里/小时的速度行驶，汽车在出口路肩上看到了 60 公里/小时的限速标志（已经被混凝土屏障与高速公路隔开，因此从技术上讲，这是一条完全不同的道路），并开始快速制动 - 我差一点就被后面的司机追上，他（理所当然）没想到我会在前面什么都没有的情况下突然开始制动。幸运的是，它可以永久关闭，这样我就可以继续使用巡航控制，而不必害怕每一个限速标志。

最近我租了一辆斯柯达卡罗克（很新的，可能是 2024/2025 年），它甚至不根据标志调整巡航控制速度，而是可能根据内置地图的数据？我不知道 - 但它会随机决定我在 90 公里/小时的道路上行驶时进入 20 公里/小时的区域。而且这个功能无法关闭。所以我完全关闭了巡航控制，因为天哪，怎么会有人认为这可以改善道路安全？

编辑：拼写错误

children.author: randerson
children.textRawHtml:
My car (from VW Group) came with Active Lane Keep. It detects when you're drifting out of your lane and steers you back into it. Sounds great in theory.

Except that in the US, if you're cruising in the right lane then every time there's an exit it will actively steer you towards the concrete median between the lane and the exit. I don't understand how this feature was signed off on. The risks outweigh the benefits, in my opinion.
children.textZhHtml:
我的汽车（来自大众集团）配备了主动车道保持功能。它会检测到您何时偏离车道并引导您返回车道。理论上听起来不错。

除了在美国，如果您在正确的车道上行驶，那么每次有出口时，它都会主动引导您驶向车道和出口之间的混凝土中间带。我不明白这个功能是如何签署的。在我看来，风险大于收益。

children.author: laughing_man
children.textRawHtml: I will never buy a car with that feature unless I can turn it off.  I don't need the be fighting my car if I need to swerve in an emergency situation.
children.textZhHtml: 我永远不会购买具有该功能的汽车，除非我可以将其关闭。  如果我需要在紧急情况下转向，我不需要与我的车战斗。

children.author: psadauskas
children.textRawHtml:
I rented a car a couple years ago, I think a Jeep of some sort (I didn't pick it), that had this feature. When driving on the highway, if there's a car in the lane next to me, I tend to move over to the other side of my lane to give more room, especially around curves. This car had lane-keeping, and would fight with me for the steering wheel, and force me uncomfortably close to the other cars, which is particularly infuriating when you're driving an unfamiliar car in an unfamiliar environment in heavy traffic at 70mph.

It only took about 5 minutes of that before I exited and looked up how to disable it, and it has to be disabled in the menus every time you start the car, there's no way to have it be permanent.

Now whenever I rent a car, I refuse to be given anything from Chrysler or VW.
children.textZhHtml:
几年前我租了一辆车，我想是某种吉普车（我没有选它），有这个功能。在高速公路上行驶时，如果我旁边的车道上有一辆车，我倾向于移至车道的另一侧以腾出更多空间，尤其是在弯道处。这辆车有车道保持功能，会和我争夺方向盘，迫使我不舒服地靠近其他车，当你在陌生的环境中以 70 英里/小时的车流量驾驶一辆陌生的车时，这尤其令人恼火。

我只花了大约 5 分钟就退出并查找如何禁用它，而且每次启动汽车时都必须在菜单中禁用它，没有办法让它被禁用永久的。

现在，每当我租车时，我都拒绝接受克莱斯勒或大众的任何东西。

children.author: doubled112
children.textRawHtml: My Toyota has a button to _enable_ lane keeping.  This is how it should be.
children.textZhHtml: 我的丰田有一个_启用_车道保持按钮。  事情应该是这样的。

children.author: jawilson2
children.textRawHtml: My Land Rover Defender has this as well.
children.textZhHtml: 我的路虎卫士也有这个问题。

children.author: zwckl
children.textRawHtml:
I drive a Volkswagen ID3 and it has the same issue. It's baffling. There are certain roads that trip it up for me and make it slam on the brakes: the A2 in Greenwich (a 50mph road in London UK) it'll suddenly think it's now 30mph, and on the M6 Northbound (70mph road in Birmingham, UK) it drops to 50mph. As far as I can see, there are no signs anywhere that the camera can misread.

On one occasion it thought the limit had changed to 100mph, which isn't even a defined speed limit in the UK.
children.textZhHtml:
我驾驶的是大众 ID3，它也有同样的问题。真是令人费解。有些道路会让我绊倒并猛踩刹车：在格林威治的 A2（英国伦敦的 50 英里/小时的道路）上，它会突然认为现在是 30 英里/小时，而在 M6 北行（英国伯明翰的 70 英里/小时的道路）上，它的速度会降至 50 英里/小时。据我所知，没有任何迹象表明摄像头可能会误读。

comments.author: A_D_E_P_T
comments.textRawHtml:
All new cars.

At this point I don't know if I'd buy anything made after 2008. Whenever I rent a new car around here (in the EU) I find them very annoying. Then there's the incessant beeping at you, "lane assist" that you can't turn off (looking at you, Volkswagen,) and many more small annoyances. A camera pointed at your face just adds insult to injury.

children.author: peterlk
children.textRawHtml:
Over Christmas, I spent several minutes trying to debug my beeping dashboard - it only seemed to happen sometimes while driving, so stopping didn’t let me figure it out. Eventually I discovered that it was beeping at me because my eyes weren’t on the road enough. Of course, figuring that out required me to take my eyes off the road to figure out which blinking signal was associated with this particular alarm.

Also, being constantly warned that I was speeding in rural areas where the car missed a speed limit sign caused me to start ignoring the speeding alarm within a few hours of driving the car.

I feel like there’s some lesson here in building to the lowest common denominator, and giving people products rather than tools (tools are more dangerous, but more useful), but maybe I’m just grumpy.

此外，不断有人警告我在农村地区超速，汽车错过了限速标志，导致我在开车几个小时内开始忽略超速警报。

我觉得这里有一些教训，可以帮助人们建立最低的共同标准，并为人们提供产品而不是工具（工具更危险，但更有效）有用），但也许我只是脾气暴躁。

children.author: psd1
children.textRawHtml:
> You can use OBD2 dongle to change default setting of lane assist

Colour me skeptical. ODBII is just a protocol; it knows nothing of the modules on your buses. I think you will find you need the dealer software. Tools exist that implement big chunks of that functionality, but since they are built on reverse-engineering, the price approaches the dealer software anyway.

The only place i found the Mercedes software was a paid forum that obscures the links. Getting the cracks working was a magic incantation. It's similar for VAG. They won't sell you the software if you're not a dealer.

You will not get there with a £40 ebay special. If you can, please share what works, it will be of much interest to many.

> 你可以使用OBD2适配器更改车道辅助的默认设置

对此我持怀疑态度。OBDII只是一种协议，它对你总线上的模块一无所知。我想你会发现你需要经销商的软件。市面上确实有一些工具能实现大部分功能，但由于它们是基于逆向工程构建的，价格反正也接近经销商软件了。

children.author: ygra
children.textRawHtml:
As a frequent contributor myself I love StreetComplete and I wouldn't worry about it perhaps generating a few changesets more than strictly necessary (it tends to make one changeset per quest (with multiple amendments for individual answers) and also only during a certain time frame. Sure, that means that adding information to a staircase creates multiple changesets (incline, step_count, surface, handrail), but that doesn't really matter.

What StreetComplete is very good at, in my opinion, is simply QA on the ground. SC asks about features existing on the map (benches, bus stops, shops, roads, barriers, etc.) and some of them might go stale. So asking about opening hours of a shop might cause someone to look and notice that the shop is closed now, or replaced by another one, so in many cases the quests that add detail to objects have the nice side-effect that those are being looked at and reviewed whether they still exist, which otherwise does not happen often. The barrier to adding new data is often lower than fixing or removing outdated information.

And it's such a low barrier to entry, especially for people not familiar with OSM's tagging, since the UX is very well designed for beginners in my eyes (while still being useful for advanced mappers), that you can easily recommend it to people who like those kind of hobbies (e.g. geocaching, Pokémon Go). For me it was an excuse to go outside more, also to places I haven't been, during COVID-19, just to tick off another quest.
children.textZhHtml:
作为一名经常贡献者，我喜欢 StreetComplete，而且我不会担心它可能会生成一些超出绝对必要的变更集（它往往会为每个任务制作一个变更集（对各个答案进行多次修改），而且也仅在特定时间范围内进行。当然，这意味着向楼梯添加信息会创建多个变更集（倾斜、步数、表面、扶手），但这并不重要。

StreetComplete 非常好在我看来，SC 只是询问地图上现有的特征（长凳、公交车站、商店、道路、障碍物等），其中一些可能会过时，因此询问商店的营业时间可能会导致某人查看并注意到该商店现在已关闭，或者被另一家商店取代，因此在许多情况下，为对象添加细节的任务会产生很好的副作用，即这些特征会被查看并审查它们是否仍然存在，否则这种情况不会经常发生。添加新数据通常比修复或删除过时的信息要低。

而且它的进入门槛很低，特别是对于不熟悉 OSM 标记的人来说，因为在我看来，用户体验非常适合初学者（同时对高级制图人员仍然有用），因此您可以轻松地将其推荐给喜欢此类爱好的人（例如寻宝、Pokémon Go），对我来说，这是一个更多外出的借口，也是我去的地方。在 COVID-19 期间，我并不是为了完成另一个任务。

children.author: maxerickson
children.textRawHtml:
The review does not block the data being added, it's more of a request for feedback.

Different maps have different update cycles. The slippy map on osm.org is meant to update as quickly as possible, usually a few minutes, but sometimes there are hiccups. Zoom levels that show larger areas also update a little slower.

As long as what you are adding is up to date/accurate, I wouldn't worry about whether you've captured everything about the features. "This thing is here" is a lot better for the map than no information.
children.textZhHtml:
审核并不阻止数据的添加，更多的是请求反馈。

不同的地图有不同的更新周期。 osm.org 上的滑动地图旨在尽快更新，通常是几分钟，但有时会出现问题。显示较大区域的缩放级别更新速度也稍慢。

只要您添加的内容是最新/准确的，我就不会担心您是否已捕获有关功能的所有内容。 “这个东西就在这里”对于地图来说比没有信息要好得多。

children.author: ygra
children.textRawHtml:
Some apps don't edit OSM directly, but rather add a note on the map for others to edit. Maybe that's what they meant.

E.g., OrganicMaps often creates these kinds of notes: https://www.openstreetmap.org/note/5377887 which for certain kinds of edits is safer than trying to directly edit. StreetComplete does the same in many cases when something no longer exists. I think, shops and other places can often be added directly, though since that has little to no ties to existing data.

But yeah, I'm using mostly StreetComplete (SCEE) when outside, sometimes Every Door and Vespucci. For offline map apps like Organic Maps it's likely not easy resolving potential conflicts with live OSM data that might be weeks or months newer than the map data in the app.
children.textZhHtml:
有些应用并不直接编辑OSM，而是在地图上添加备注，交由他人来编辑。也许他们指的就是这个。

例如，OrganicMaps经常会创建这类备注：https://www.openstreetmap.org/note/5377887，对于某些类型的编辑来说，这比直接编辑更安全。StreetComplete在很多情况下也是如此，比如当某个地点已不存在时。我认为，商店和其他地点通常可以直接添加，因为这几乎与现有数据没有关联。

不过确实，我在户外主要使用StreetComplete（SCEE），有时也用Every Door和Vespucci。对于像Organic Maps这样的离线地图应用来说，解决与实时OSM数据的潜在冲突可能并不容易，因为实时数据可能比应用内的地图数据新几周甚至几个月。

comments.author: HelloUsername
comments.textRawHtml:
Interesting comment from last time this was posted https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48093670

Inkjet printing requires orders of magnitude more engineering expertise, materials science, industry experience and financial resources than most people imagine. That is the reason, open inkjet printers don't exist despite having been consumer products with the same drawbacks for more than forty years. That is why this is a pre-crowdfund landing page without a demonstrating a working prototype. I would like to be wrong, but I expect you to be waiting a long time. An inkjet printer is not a collection of off the shelf parts. It is a machine that operates at the edge of chemistry, fluid dynamics, and electro-mechanical design...you have to place tiny tiny drops of liquid ink on commodity wood pulp with precision under arbitrary environmental conditions, get that ink to dry on the wood pulp, but not in tank or nozzel, while producing acceptable color, durability, and ease of use. Also lawyers...there are patents.
comments.textZhHtml:
上次发这篇帖子时的一条有趣评论 https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48093670

喷墨打印技术所需的工程专业知识、材料科学素养、行业经验以及资金资源，比大多数人想象的要高出几个数量级。正因如此，尽管喷墨打印机作为消费级产品存在同样的缺陷已有四十多年，但开源喷墨打印机至今仍未问世。 这就是为什么目前这只是一个众筹前的预热页面，而没有展示可运行的原型机。我真心希望自己的判断是错的，但我估计大家还要等上很长一段时间。 喷墨打印机绝非现成零部件的简单拼凑。它是一台游走在化学、流体动力学和机电设计极限边缘的精密设备……你必须在各种复杂的环境条件下，将极其微小的液态墨滴精准地喷射到普通木浆纸上，并确保墨水能在纸上干燥，而不会在墨盒或喷嘴中干涸，同时还要保证色彩表现、耐久性和易用性均达到合格标准。 还有律师……毕竟有专利壁垒。

children.author: tjohns
children.textRawHtml:
On the technology side, I'm somewhat hopeful because it looks like they're using off-the-shelf HP ink cartridges for this. HP cartridges embed the printhead into the cartridge itself, and that printhead is arguably the most complicated part of the entire device. Outsource the printhead, and you're just designing a plotter with a PCL interface.

I agree that the bigger challenge is going to be patents.

It also wouldn't surprise me to see HP add DRM to cartridges to authenticate the printer itself if this catches on. (Possibly requiring a printer driver/firmware update.)
children.textZhHtml:
在技​​术方面，我有些希望，因为看起来他们正在使用现成的惠普墨盒。惠普墨盒将打印头嵌入墨盒本身，打印头可以说是整个设备中最复杂的部分。将打印头外包，您只需设计带有 PCL 接口的绘图仪即可。

我同意，更大的挑战将在于专利。

如果这一趋势蔓延开来，惠普在墨盒中加入DRM来验证打印机本身，我也不会感到意外。（可能需要更新打印机驱动程序或固件。）

children.author: londons_explore
children.textRawHtml:
The cartridge heads have a super curious digital protocol - the cartridge electronics have no power supply. There is no power rail internally either.

Instead they use the fact mosfet gates have capacitance, and can therefore store 1 'bit' of information. Through a network of hundreds of MOSFETs, the right bits can be put on all the nozzle gates as needed, and then a 'fire' pulse is sent which for around a microsecond turns on a tiny heater (or not, depending on mosfet gate state). The heater boils some ink, making a pressure wave which travels down a pipe and makes a drop of ink fly out towards the paper. That heating and cooling again can happen around 10,000 times per second per nozzle (and there are ~300 nozzles).

I suspect this decision is because the custom silicon process needed to manufacture these nozzles is cheaper if they only have n type MOSFETs - and without p type MOSFETs you can't make a typical push pull logic gate.

The nozzles themselves are quite a lot of silicon - perhaps 100mm^2, with deep etched holes, and are effectively disposable, so I assume huge efforts have been taken to reduce costs - including this curious electrical design.

The protocol also is hugely irregular, which I suspect might be to avoid any wires on the chip needing to cross eachother.
children.textZhHtml:
墨盒喷头采用了一种极其奇特的数字协议——墨盒电子元件 无电源内部也没有电源轨。

相反，他们利用的是MOSFET栅极具有电容这一特性，从而能够存储1“比特”的信息。通过由数百个MOSFET组成的网络，可以根据需要将所有喷嘴的栅极设置为相应的数据位，随后发送一个“点火”脉冲，该脉冲会在约一微秒的时间内接通微型加热器（是否接通取决于MOSFET栅极的状态）。加热器会沸腾少量墨水，产生压力波，该压力波沿管道传递，促使一滴墨水喷射向纸张。这种加热与冷却的循环过程在每个喷嘴上每秒可发生约10,000次（而总共有约300个喷嘴）。

我推测这一决定是因为，制造这些喷嘴所需的定制硅工艺在仅使用N型MOSFET时成本更低——而且如果没有P型MOSFET，就无法制造典型的推挽逻辑门。

喷嘴本身耗用了相当多的硅材料——面积或许达100平方毫米，带有深刻蚀孔，实际上属于一次性消耗品，因此我推测为降低成本必定付出了巨大努力——其中就包括这种奇特的电气设计。

该协议也极其不规范，我怀疑这可能是为了避免芯片上的导线相互交叉。

children.author: hyperbrainer
children.textRawHtml: This sounds fascinating. Do you have a reference?
children.textZhHtml: 这听起来非常有趣。请问您有相关的参考资料吗？

children.author: bri3d
children.textRawHtml:
Impression v. Lexmark is directly relevant to the patent situation here; in a case involving reprogrammed ink cartridges, the Supreme Court held that patent rights are exhausted by first sale doctrine; that is, if you sell me something patented, you can't sue me for patent infringement for using the thing you sold me, even if I do something later that makes you upset.

It's also been widely held that ink cartridge compatibility tools/hacks are allowed under DMCA.

However, it does seem likely that there's probably some shenanigan involved in the area - one example I could think of would be if HP have patented the way the ink cartridges are retained in the printer, for example, that would have to be carefully audited. And there will be license and trade secrets issues with the use of the cartridges, although if they were reverse engineered cleanly and the printer doesn't come with cartridges, those are probably pretty easily side-stepped.
children.textZhHtml:
印象产品诉利盟案与这里的专利情况直接相关；在涉及重新编程墨盒的案件中，最高法院裁定，适用首次销售原则即专利权用尽；也就是说，如果你将专利产品卖给我，你就不能因为我使用你卖给我的产品而起诉我专利侵权，哪怕我后来做了让你不满的事情。

人们还普遍认为，DMCA 允许使用墨盒兼容性工具/黑客。

然而，这个领域似乎很可能存在一些猫腻——我能想到的一个例子是，如果惠普对墨盒在打印机中的固定方式申请了专利，那就需要仔细审查。而且墨盒的使用还会涉及许可和商业秘密问题，不过，如果逆向工程做得干净利落，且打印机本身不附带墨盒，这些问题大概很容易就能规避。

children.author: londons_explore
children.textRawHtml:
These cartridges were first released in 2004, and there don't seem to have been any major design changes since then. The electrical and digital interface for printing has remained the same, as well as the mechanics. There is a new DRM system using another chip, but that is one-way - ie. It prevents an original printer from using the cartridge, but doesn't prevent use of an original cartridge by a third party printer.

Any patents are probably expired or very close to it.
children.textZhHtml:
这些墨盒于 2004 年首次发布，此后似乎没有任何重大的设计变化。 用于打印的电气和数字接口以及机械结构保持不变。 有一个新的 DRM 系统使用另一个芯片，但这是单向的 - 即。它会阻止原装打印机使用墨盒，但不会阻止第三方打印机使用原装墨盒。

任何专利都可能已过期或即将过期。

children.author: Arainach
children.textRawHtml:
In medicine, it's a regular trick to find some new patentable formulation or tweak to switch to manufacturing shortly before the original patents expire, so that you continue to have a patent-protected product.

It would not surprise me if something similar was happening here: HP's current cartridges have at least one piece that is under active patent protection and they don't manufacture any designs that have no patent protection. I don't know enough to state that's the case, but it wouldn't surprise me.
children.textZhHtml:
在医学领域，在原始专利到期前不久找到一些新的可专利配方或进行调整以转为制造是一种常见的技巧，这样您就可以继续拥有受专利保护的产品。

如果这里发生类似的情况，我不会感到惊讶：惠普当前的墨盒至少有一个受到有效专利保护的部件，并且他们不生产任何没有专利保护的设计。 我不太了解情况，但我不会感到惊讶。

children.author: alnwlsn
children.textRawHtml:
Mentioned elsewhere in this thread, but the print heads are a very specialized silicon chip with internal microfluidic passages, microscopic heaters, etc. Who else would even make them? The kind of thing you would need millions of dollars to reverse engineer and replicate just to enter the lucrative market of... making inkjet cartridges.

I would be shocked to discover that there are counterfeit print heads out there the same way there are counterfeit STM32 microcontrollers.

Unless the patents have something to do with the shape of the cartridge or they somehow have a patent on "technique to fill printer cartridge with ink" which would not be so surprising.
children.textZhHtml:

我会惊讶地发现那里有假冒的打印头，就像有假冒的 STM32 微控制器一样。

除非这些专利与墨盒的形状有关，或者他们以某种方式拥有“向打印机墨盒填充墨水的技术”的专利，否则这并不奇怪。

children.author: ssl-3
children.textRawHtml:
I did some research on weird inkjet printers a few years ago. We wanted to do stuff like print white[1] graphics continuously onto rolls of black heat shrink tubing.

One thing I discovered was that there's whole Chinese industries supplying this stuff in the forms of industrial printers and the application-specific cartridges they consume. And they all had one thing in common: They were based around the functionality of an HP printer cartridge. That was the universal interface.

This leads me to believe that a print cartridge format can be supported by the marketplace for a very long time whether HP likes it or not.

[1]: Printing with white is neat. It sounds impossible and there are those who say it can't happen, but it exists. It's just problematic. The head gets perma-clogged with solids within minutes of disuse in open air. So starting a print job is a race: Get everything set up, uncap the cartridge at the last moment, and then put that cap back on ASAP when the job is over.
children.textZhHtml:
几年前我对奇怪的喷墨打印机进行了一些研究。 我们想要做一些事情，比如在黑色热缩管卷上连续打印白色[1]图形。

我发现的一件事是，整个中国工业都以工业打印机及其消耗的特定应用墨盒的形式提供这种东西。 它们都有一个共同点：它们都是基于 HP 打印机墨盒的功能。 这就是通用接口。

这让我相信，无论惠普是否喜欢，打印墨盒格式都可以在市场上长期支持。

[1]：用白色打印很整洁。 这听起来不可能，有人说这不可能发生，但它确实存在。 这只是有问题的。 在露天闲置后的分钟内，头部就会被固体永久堵塞。 因此，开始打印作业就像一场竞赛：把所有东西都设置好，在最后一刻打开墨盒盖，然后在作业结束时尽快重新盖上盖子。

children.author: saturn8601
children.textRawHtml:
Are you referring to the numerous Chinese UV printers?

>The head gets perma-clogged with solids within minutes of disuse in open air. So starting a print job is a race: Get everything set up, uncap the cartridge at the last moment, and then put that cap back on ASAP when the job is over

Decades ago I helped manage a local's school IT systems and we had these Epson C84 printers that used a very photo grade ink. The printheads would clog after a year of teachers printing like 1 document in that year. During that time it seemed like regular purging of ink regardless of print or not was what kept the printheads going. Strategies used by third party repair firms included submerging the head in a cleaner to dissolve the ink on the outside, connecting pipes with the same fluid to the inputs of the top to the printhead and then finally pulling pressure to try and dislodge the material through the head. Due to the non removable design of the Epson print heads, my attempts at trying this would destroy many printheads. I'd like to think this would be a solved problem in 2026. In your case why not run a purge liquid the printhead after each print? The printhead could then be primed with fresh ink each time.
children.textZhHtml:
你说的是那些众多的中国产UV打印机吗？

>喷头在空气中闲置几分钟就会被固体物永久堵塞。所以开始打印简直就是一场赛跑：把一切都准备好，在最后一刻才打开墨盒盖，然后一打完就赶紧把盖子盖回去。

几十年前，我帮忙管理当地学校的IT系统，我们用的是爱普生C84打印机，用的是非常高级的照片级墨水。老师们一年到头也就打印个一份文件，结果喷头用了一年就堵了。那时候看来，不管打不打印，定期清洗墨水才是保持喷头正常工作的关键。第三方维修公司用的策略包括：把喷头泡在清洗液里溶解外面的墨水，用管子把同样的液体连到喷头顶部的输入口，最后施加负压试图把堵塞物从喷头里抽出来。由于爱普生喷头是不可拆卸的设计，我尝试这些方法弄坏了好多喷头。我本以为到2026年这个问题早就解决了。像你这种情况，为什么不在每次打印后用清洗液冲洗一下喷头呢？这样每次都可以用新墨水给喷头打底。

children.author: ssl-3
children.textRawHtml:
I don't know what a UV printer is. :)

We were using fixed-head printers that we found at the bottom of a deep rabbit hole. The work moved through the machine while the printy-bits remained stationary. We were doing this with HP-style monochromatic carts that came loaded with high-solids "ink" (closer to paint, really) that used opaque white pigment.

Unlike Epson's ecosystem, HP carts most-commonly come as assemblies, with a new print head included in the same disposable cartridge as the ink is. The only connection between the carts and the rest of the printer is electrical; there's no plumbing or functional fluids that are external to the carts.

This self-contained nature means that when ink runs out or heads gets clogged, replacement of everything involved with fluids is fast and easy. Production can continue immediately.

It's not problematic at all...other than being expensive. :)
children.textZhHtml:
我不知道UV打印机是什么。:)

我们用的是在某个极深坑底找到的固定头打印机。材料在机器里移动，而打印部件保持静止。我们用的是HP风格的单色墨盒，里面装满了高固含量的“墨水”（其实更像颜料），用的是不透明的白色颜料。

与爱普生的生态系统不同，HP墨盒通常是一体化组件，新喷头和墨水装在同一个一次性墨盒里。墨盒和打印机其余部分之间唯一的连接就是电路；墨盒外部没有任何管道或功能性液体。

这种自包含的特性意味着，当墨水用完或喷头堵塞时，更换所有涉及液体的部件既快又容易。生产可以立即继续。

这完全不是问题……除了太贵。:)

comments.author: ikut3hva
comments.textRawHtml:
Hi everyone

I don’t usually comment on topics like this because there are so many biases and different perspectives involved. In the end, I believe only the person who has actually gone through the experience can truly understand it; otherwise, it often becomes just another judgment.

We are an ASEAN family earning more than €200k gross annually (sorry for mentioning the TC, but there is a reason for it—please keep reading before judging). We have lived here for more than six years, and you know what? I still haven’t obtained either permanent residence or German citizenship simply because I don’t have a B1 certificate. So first things first: regardless of how much you contribute to the country, German is a must today if you want to obtain residency and stabilize your life here.

I was honestly devastated when the officer told me that I was not eligible for permanent residence. That was also the moment when I started to feel that maybe I don’t actually need permanent residence in this country after all.

Story 2: In an international working environment, German may not matter much at the IC level. But I’ve seen countless situations where Germans exchange a glance with each other, and suddenly the final decision is not what was agreed upon in the meeting. Over time, I’ve learned that there are many unwritten rules behind the scenes, and when you speak their language, you start to understand them.

One bright thing is that maybe we’re still lucky. We bought our first home without fully understanding the laws, the government system, or the tax rules. We simply worked hard and played the game in a way that we believed would be sustainable in the long run. Whatever happens, we know there are still many other places we could go.

Our children speak German natively, but they are also willing to go the extra mile to speak our mother tongue at home.

If you ask me for one piece of advice for immigrants and emigrants in Germany, I’d say: life is short—play naked!
comments.textZhHtml:
大家好

我通常不会评论这样的话题，因为其中涉及很多偏见和不同的观点。最后，我相信只有真正经历过的人才能真正理解；否则，它往往会成为另一个判断。

我们是一个年收入超过20万欧元的东盟家庭（很抱歉提到TC，但这是有原因的——请在判断之前继续阅读）。我们在这里住了六年多了，你知道吗？我仍然没有获得永久居留权或德国公民身份，只是因为我没有 B1 证书。因此，首先要做的事情是：无论您为国家做出了多少贡献，如果您想获得居留权并在这里稳定生活，德语都是必须的。

当官员告诉我我没有资格获得永久居留权时，我真的感到很沮丧。也是在那一刻，我开始觉得也许我实际上并不需要在这个国家永久居留。

故事 2：在国际工作环境中，德语在 IC 层面上可能并不重要。但我见过无数次这样的情况：德国人互相交换了一个眼神，突然间最终的决定并不是会议上达成的一致。随着时间的推移，我了解到幕后有许多不成文的规则，当你说他们的语言时，你开始理解它们。

一件好事是，也许我们仍然很幸运。我们在没有完全了解法律、政府制度或税收规则的情况下购买了第一套房子。我们只是努力工作，并以我们认为从长远来看可持续的方式玩游戏。无论发生什么，我们知道我们还有很多其他地方可以去。

我们的孩子的母语是德语，但他们也愿意在家里多花点力气说我们的母语。

如果你问我对德国的移民和移民有什么建议，我会说：生命短暂——赤身裸体！

children.author: dgs_sgd
children.textRawHtml:
I appreciate your perspective, but I was curious what B1 proficiency actually entails and this is what I found [1]:

- understand the main points of clear standard speech on familiar topics such as work, school, or leisure - manage most situations that occur while traveling in German-speaking areas - produce simple, connected text on familiar subjects - describe experiences, events, dreams, hopes, and ambitions, and briefly explain your opinions or plans

That seems like a reasonable standard of native language proficiency to ask of people who want to make the county with said language their permanent home.

[1] https://www.sprachenatelier-berlin.de/en/topic/3736.german-p...
children.textZhHtml:
我很欣赏你的观点，但我很好奇 B1 熟练程度实际上意味着什么，这就是我发现的 [1]：

- 理解关于工作、学校或休闲等熟悉主题的清晰标准演讲的要点 - 管理在德语地区旅行时发生的大多数情况 - 就熟悉的主题生成简单、相互关联的文本 - 描述经历、事件、梦想、希望和抱负，并简要解释您的意见或计划

对于那些想要将使用该语言的县作为永久家园的人来说，这似乎是一个合理的母语熟练程度标准。

children.author: TheOtherHobbes
children.textRawHtml:
B1 is a completely fair minimum standard. It's normal for many countries to expect residents to have basic conversational adequacy.

It's also the kind of requirement that's made explicit on government information about residency. So it shouldn't have been a surprise.
children.textZhHtml:
B1 是一个完全公平的最低标准。对于许多国家来说，期望居民具备基本的会话能力是很正常的。

这也是政府对居住信息提出的明确要求。所以这并不奇怪。

children.author: DiskoHexyl
children.textRawHtml:
It is completely fair to expect at least B1 from potential citizens and permanent residents.

What the article is covering are the skilled workers, and for them B1 isn't nearly enough to function in any profesional capacity. I'd put B2 as a bare minimum for that. So if it's impossible for someone to find employment in the language they already know (for skilled workers it's usually English), they are understandably not eager to stay.

For those who are quick to judge, try mastering a foreign language (and German is noticeably more difficult than English, French or Spanish) in another country where you don't have any support system yet, and with likely some suboptimal job (as you need this foreign language to find a decent one). Getting to B2 takes about 750 class hours (not including homework) for an average English speaker. That's an enormous investment (of both time and money). One will have to sacrifice entertainment, socializing, hobbies, sleep, and work-related educational opportunities for several years in a row, depending on abilities (and the language).

And just a reminder- most people tend to overestimate how well they know a foreign language. Telling you know [insert your flavor of Chinese] and actually passing an exam are two different things.

Again, I am not saying it is unfair for country officials to expect whatever language level they want. It is their country and their rules. As far as I understand nobody's talking about giving away passports.

It's just when it's relatively more difficult for skilled immigrants to find employment and interact with said officials without high level (at least B2) German, some of these immigrants will chose in favor of the countries- with easier/more widely used languages and/or more jobs in English or just lower taxes and higher pay.

If a country's government wants to attract these kinds of immigrants, some of these variables will have to change
children.textZhHtml:
对潜在公民和永久居民的期望至少达到 B1 是完全公平的。

本文涵盖的是技术工人，对他们来说，B1 不足以发挥任何专业能力。我认为 B2 是最低限度。因此，如果某人不可能用他们已经掌握的语言找到工作（对于技术工人来说通常是英语），他们就不急于留下来，这是可以理解的。

对于那些快速判断的人来说，尝试在另一个国家掌握一门外语（德语明显比英语、法语或西班牙语更难），在那里你还没有任何支持系统，并且可能有一些次优的工作（因为你需要这门外语才能找到一份体面的工作）。 对于一个普通的说英语的人来说，达到 B2 水平大约需要 750 个课时（不包括家庭作业）。这是一项巨大的投资（时间和金钱）。根据能力（和语言），一个人将不得不连续几年牺牲娱乐、社交、爱好、睡眠和与工作相关的教育机会。

提醒一下，大多数人往往会高估自己对一门外语的了解程度。告诉你知道[插入你的中文风格]和实际通过考试是两件不同的事情。

再说一遍，我并不是说国家官员期望他们想要的语言水平是不公平的。这是他们的国家和他们的规则。据我所知，没有人在谈论放弃护照。

只是当技术移民找工作并与没有高水平（至少 B2）德语的上述官员互动相对更加困难时，其中一些移民会选择有利于语言更容易/更广泛使用和/或更多英语工作的国家，或者只是更低的税收和更高的工资。

如果一个国家的政府想要吸引这类移民，其中一些变量必须改变

children.author: dgs_sgd
children.textRawHtml: Like, I imagine if I moved to a country and couldn’t do the B1 things in their language, I would have daily obstacles doing basic life things.
children.textZhHtml: 就像，我想象如果我搬到一个国家并且无法用他们的语言做 B1 的事情，我每天做基本生活的事情都会遇到障碍。

children.author: 14u2c
children.textRawHtml:
Not in most of Western and Central Europe. Everyone speaks great English.

It actually can be a problem because most people switch to English as soon as they realize your are not a native speaker, which can make learning harder.
children.textZhHtml:
西欧和中欧的大部分地区没有。每个人的英语都说得很好。

这实际上可能是一个问题，因为大多数人一意识到您不是母语者就改用英语，这会让学习变得更加困难。

children.author: marcus_holmes
children.textRawHtml:
This was my experience. I lived for almost four years in Berlin, and was trying to learn German (I got to A2, which isn't much). But actually getting experience/immersion in German is difficult in Berlin - everyone under the age of 50 speaks good English and switched to that as soon as my first badly-conjugated sentence left my mouth.

I worked for two German companies while I was there, and the entire tech team in both companies did everything in English. This is normal there. In one of them, the (German) CEO gave a townhall speech to the entire company (~1000 employees) in English.

So yes, it's very understandable for the German government to say "you must be at least B1 to live here" but that doesn't recognise the fact that it is perfectly possible to actually live and work in Berlin with no German at all.

It's different in other German cities, apparently. If we'd moved to Munich or Cologne I would have had more pressure to learn German, and also more exposure to it and more immersion which would have helped learn it.
children.textZhHtml:
这是我的经历。我在柏林住了将近四年，一直在努力学习德语（我达到了 A2，这并不多）。但实际上在柏林获得德语的经验/沉浸式学习是很困难的 - 每个 50 岁以下的人都会说一口流利的英语，当我说出第一句话时，他们就转向英语。

我在那里时曾为两家德国公司工作过，两家公司的整个技术团队都用英语做所有事情。这在那里很正常。在其中一篇文章中，（德国）首席执行官用英语向整个公司（约 1000 名员工）发表了市政厅演讲。

所以，是的，德国政府说“你必须至少达到 B1 才能住在这里”是可以理解的，但这并没有认识到一个事实，即完全没有德语也完全有可能在柏林生活和工作。

显然，这在其他德国城市是不同的。如果我们搬到慕尼黑或科隆，我会面临更大的学习德语的压力，也会有更多的接触德语和更多的沉浸感，这将有助于学习德语。

children.author: thayne
children.textRawHtml:
Even when I vacationed in Bayern (Bavaria), a lot of people switched to English when they realized that my German wasn't that good. Which was frustrating, because I wanted to practice speaking with native speakers.

Although, there were also people who only spoke German.
children.textZhHtml:
即使当我在拜仁（巴伐利亚）度假时，很多人在意识到我的德语不太好时也改用英语。这很令人沮丧，因为我想练习与母语人士交谈。

尽管如此，也有人只说德语。

children.author: doikor
children.textRawHtml: If you want to learn you just pretend that you don’t speak English and keep on speaking German. They will switch back fast.
children.textZhHtml: 如果你想学习，你就假装你不会说英语，然后继续说德语。他们会很快切换回来。

children.author: flakeoil
children.textRawHtml:
This is the right way. Speak the language you want. Even if they reply to you in English, and they know you can speak English, continue in German. They will understand. They switch to English because they want to be helpful. If you continue in German they understand you prefer it that way.

Another thing is that once you start talking with a person (who you meet on a regular basis) in one language, it is hard to switch to another language at a later date. So if you start with English, it is hard to go back to German. But if you start with German, even if it is less fluent for you, it will come natural that with that person you speak in German.
children.textZhHtml:
这是正确的方法。说您想要的语言。即使他们用英语回复您，并且他们知道您会说英语，也请继续用德语。他们会理解的。他们改用英语是因为他们想提供帮助。如果您继续使用德语，他们会理解您更喜欢这种方式。

另一件事是，一旦您开始用一种语言与某人（您经常遇到的人）交谈，以后就很难切换到另一种语言。所以如果你从英语开始，就很难再回到德语。但如果你从德语开始，即使你不太流利，你也会很自然地和那个人说德语。

children.author: throwaway2037
children.textRawHtml:
Did you ask if you can move to a different city and work remotely? Even if only 1-2 years, it might really help to boost your German. Moving to Dresden, Hanover, or Hamburg would help. (German HNers can probably recommend a bunch of small cities 1-2 hours away by train/car from Berlin that will use much more German language!) You could come to the office once a week.

Another tip: Always start the conversation in German. If the person switches to English, ask them politely to speak German, and explain that you are studying German. My parents did similar when they moved to the Netherlands 50 years ago. The local shopkeepers were eager to practice English with my parents, but my parents were also eager to practice their Dutch.
children.textZhHtml:
您是否询问过是否可以搬到其他城市并远程工作？ 即使只有 1-2 年，也可能确实有助于提高您的德语。 搬到德累斯顿、汉诺威或汉堡会有帮助。 （德国人可能会推荐一些距离柏林 1-2 小时火车/汽车车程的小城市，这些城市会使用更多德语！）您可以每周来办公室一次。

另一个提示：始终用德语开始对话。 如果对方改用英语，请礼貌地请他们说德语，并解释说您正在学习德语。 50 年前，我的父母搬到荷兰时也做了类似的事情。 当地的店主很想和我的父母一起练习英语，但我的父母也很想练习荷兰语。

children.author: marcus_holmes
children.textRawHtml:
That's really good advice, but I found it hard to put into practice because my German was so bad. And German isn't a language you can just hammer out a badly-formed sentence in; word position is less important than conjugation, so if you get the conjugation wrong it makes no sense at all to the listener. I did try this a few times, but everyone lost patience with the painful butchering of their language when they realised their English was a lot better than my German.

I would have moved city, but a: COVID b: my wife also had a job there c: I really loved Berlin and really enjoyed living there
children.textZhHtml:
这确实是一个很好的建议，但我发现很难付诸实践，因为我的德语太糟糕了。德语不是一种你可以敲出一个结构错误的句子的语言；词位不如词形变化重要，所以如果词形变化错误，听者就毫无意义。我确实尝试过几次，但当每个人意识到他们的英语比我的德语好得多时，他们都对语言的痛苦屠宰失去了耐心。

我本来会搬到城市，但是a：新冠病毒b：我妻子在那里也有一份工作c：我真的很喜欢柏林，真的很喜欢在那里生活

children.author: throwaway2037
children.textRawHtml: I like the effort! It is more that half of the battle when learning a new language as an adult. What really helped me: Create your own custom Anki deck. Literally, start from zero. Each time you try-and-fail (or think of something that you don't know how to say), write a note to yourself in English/broken German with the sentence/idea that failed. At home, use a translate tool (LLM/Google/whatever) to build the correct German sentence. Next, add it to your Anki deck. Personally, I only add full sentences, never just vocab, because it adds more natural context. Only add real stuff that you will use. On the first day, you have an Anki deck with one card. If you are really motivated, try to add one card per day, but just 1-2 cards per week will quickly improve the deck. Since the deck is so damn small, you can blow through it in five minutes while riding the S-Bahn/U-Bahn trains. And, every single sentence is useful to you. Bonus: Ask a native German speaker about some of your Anki deck sentences. They will probably love to talk to you about the German language because you will be showing your commitment.
children.textZhHtml: 我喜欢这种努力！ 作为一个成年人，学习一门新语言就已经成功了一半。 真正帮助我的是：创建您的自己的自定义 Anki 牌组。 顾名思义，就是从零开始。 每次你尝试失败（或者想到一些你不知道怎么说的事情）时，用英语/蹩脚的德语给自己写一张纸条，写上失败的句子/想法。 在家里，使用翻译工具（法学硕士/谷歌/其他）来构建正确的德语句子。 接下来，将其添加到您的 Anki 套牌中。 就我个人而言，我只添加完整的句子，而不仅仅是词汇，因为它添加了更自然的上下文。 仅添加您将使用的真实内容。 第一天，您将拥有一套包含一张卡片的 Anki 套牌。 如果您确实有动力，请尝试每天添加一张卡，但每周只需添加 1-2 张卡即可快速改善牌组。 由于甲板实在是太小了，乘坐 S-Bahn/U-Bahn 火车只需五分钟就可以穿过它。 而且，每一句话对你都有用。 额外奖励：向以德语为母语的人询问您的 Anki 甲板句子。 他们可能会很乐意与您谈论德语，因为您会表现出您的承诺。

children.author: bryanrasmussen
children.textRawHtml:
>everyone under the age of 50 speaks good English and switched to that as soon as my first badly-conjugated sentence left my mouth.

OK maybe things have changed in recent years. I was always annoyed when coming to visit family living in Berlin for a couple days or driving in to a conference, and inevitably there would come a time when I would have to ask someone for help and I would have to ask in English because I do not speak German (although due to youthful immersion I start to remember it again after a couple of weeks) and they would inevitably answer in German.

I could somewhat understand when we stopped in a park asking where the convention center was of just a couple people by the side of the road, but asking a ticket agent at a train station what route I should take to get to a particular station and getting replies in German seemed especially rude.

At any rate despite some good experiences with Germans regarding helpfulness I have had a few too many negative experiences.
children.textZhHtml:
>每个 50 岁以下的人都会说一口流利的英语，当我说出第一句话时，我就开始说英语了。

好吧，也许近年来情况已经发生了变化。当我来柏林探望几天的家人或开车去参加会议时，我总是很恼火，不可避免地会有这样的时候，我必须向某人寻求帮助，我必须用英语询问，因为我不会说德语（尽管由于年轻的沉浸感，几周后我又开始记住它），他们不可避免地会用德语回答。

当我们在公园停下来询问会议中心在哪里时，旁边只有几个人，我可以理解。路，但在火车站询问售票员我应该走哪条路线才能到达特定车站并得到德语答复似乎特别粗鲁。

无论如何，尽管德国人在帮助方面有一些良好的经验，但我还是有过太多负面的经历。

children.author: marcus_holmes
children.textRawHtml:
Berliners are renowned even in Germany for being blunt to the point of rudeness.

What we in the Anglosphere would consider rude, they consider efficient. It can be very off-putting at first, but you do get used to it.
children.textZhHtml:
即使在德国，柏林人也因直率到粗鲁而闻名。

我们在英语圈中认为粗鲁的事情，在他们看来却是高效的。一开始可能会非常令人不快，但你确实会习惯它。

children.author: bryanrasmussen
children.textRawHtml: in most other countries renowned for being efficient it would not be considered efficient to answer in a language the questioner evidently does not speak.
children.textZhHtml: 在大多数其他以高效着称的国家，用提问者显然不会说的语言回答不会被认为是高效的。

children.author: aveao
children.textRawHtml:
This isn't really true. I've lived in both Munich and Hamburg now, and you 100% need some amount of german to navigate life, many people do not in fact have conversational-level english. I often say that the common paths tend to be easy to navigate in english (e.g. booking a train ticket, getting a phone line), but the adjacent flows (e.g. filing a refund request for your unfulfilled seat reservation, cancelling your phone contract) often require talking to someone in german. A doctor may be marked as speaking english, but that doesn't mean that their reception speaks english.

Berlin may be a different story within Germany, and then there's the entirety of e.g. Netherlands, but I know that the situation in France isn't better than here.

---

That said, B1 is a difficult level within german. Being able to converse fluently enough in not-quite-correct german -which is enough for getting through day to day life- is not necessarily enough to pass the test, and you really need to crunch through memorizing the grammatical gender of thousands of words, alongside getting really good at applying them in various grammatical forms (e.g. declensions vary for the same word based on how it's used).
children.textZhHtml:
这不是真的。我现在住在慕尼黑和汉堡，你100%需要一定程度的德语来驾驭生活，很多人实际上没有会话水平的英语。我经常说，常见的路径往往很容易用英语导航（例如预订火车票、拨打电话线），但相邻的流程（例如为未完成的座位预订提出退款请求、取消电话合同）通常需要与用德语的人交谈。医生可能被标记为说英语，但这并不意味着他们的接待处说英语。

在德国柏林可能是一个不同的故事，然后还有整个例如。荷兰，但我知道法国的情况并不比这里好。

---

话虽如此，B1在德语中是一个困难的级别。能够用不完全正确的德语进行足够流利的交谈（这足以应付日常生活）并不一定足以通过测试，你确实需要记住数千个单词的语法性别，同时真正擅长以各种语法形式应用它们（例如，同一个单词的词尾变化根据其使用方式而有所不同）。

children.author: stnikolauswagne
children.textRawHtml:
There is some sort of weird phenomenon that I cant quite put to words, but the core is:

While there is a big chunk of the native german population speaking great english there is a subset of (often) service workers who... dont. Had multiple furniture movers come through my house the last few weeks, the only way to communicate with them was broken german, since they had no english skills and my (croatian? serbian?) skills were zero.

At one point I ended up in the weirdest situation of my live were i translated the german instructions from the phone rep into english to the shift lead of the moving company so he could then give them in the native language to the movers.
children.textZhHtml:
有一种奇怪的现象，我无法用语言来形容，但其核心是：

虽然有很大一部分德国本土人口能说流利的英语，但有一小部分（通常）服务人员......却不会。过去几周，有多名家具搬运工来到我家，与他们沟通的唯一方式是蹩脚的德语，因为他们没有英语技能，而我的（克罗地亚语？塞尔维亚语？）技能为零。

有一次，我陷入了一生中最奇怪的境地，我将电话代表的德语指令翻译成英语，交给搬家公司的值班领导，这样他就可以用母语将它们交给搬运工。

children.author: bulbar
children.textRawHtml:
That's basically why you should learn the language of the country you live in.

Blaming people for not speaking English in Germany is pretty weird. How about learning German instead?
children.textZhHtml:
这就是为什么你应该学习你所居住国家的语言的原因。

在德国指责人们不会说英语是很奇怪的。学习德语怎么样？

children.author: stnikolauswagne
children.textRawHtml: I speak german as a mothertongue and fluent english, and I had massive troubles communicating in my own home.
children.textZhHtml: 我的母语是德语，英语也很流利，但我在自己家里沟通时遇到了很大的困难。

stories.title: GLM 5.2 and the coming AI margin collapse
stories.titleZh: GLM 5.2 和即将到来的人工智能利润率崩溃

comments.author: fny
comments.textRawHtml:
I'm not convinced raw costs matter:

1. Compute costs collapsed since the advent of Cloud and yet hyperscalers still have fat margins.

2. Many open source office suites exist yet none compete with the ubiquity of gsuite or office. GitHub, Slack are similar examples.

3. Both Windows and macOS dominate the home desktop space despite free alternatives existing for a long time.

4. Many formerly open source infrastructure components like Redis and Elastic Search have Apache equivalents, but they still command healthy margins.

I understand the arguments for a margin collapse, but I don't see any historical analogues. It seems that enterprises will pay top dollar for service guarantees, integration, and someone they can sue.

It's nobody gets fired for buying IBM all over again.
comments.textZhHtml:
我不相信原始成本很重要：

1.自云出现以来，计算成本大幅下降，但超大规模企业仍然拥有丰厚的利润。

2.存在许多开源办公套件，但没有一个可以与无处不在的 gsuite 或 Office 竞争。 GitHub、Slack 都是类似的例子。

3.尽管免费替代品早已存在，但 Windows 和 macOS 都在家庭桌面领域占据主导地位。

4.许多以前的开源基础设施组件（如 Redis 和 Elastic Search）都有 Apache 的同类组件，但它们仍然拥有健康的利润。

我理解利润崩溃的论点，但我没有看到任何历史类似物。企业似乎愿意为服务保证、集成以及可以起诉的人支付高额费用。

没有人会因为再次购买 IBM 而被解雇。

children.author: nostrademons
children.textRawHtml:
There's a huge case of survivorship bias when trying to recall historical analogues, because in every instance where margins collapsed and competition made the industry a commodity business, the big proprietary names are no longer with us. Here's a selection of examples, though:

1. Memory chip margins collapsed so much in the 80s that Intel exited the memory chip business entirely. At the time, they were known much more as a memory chip company than a microprocessor company.

2. Margins for high-end workstations collapsed in the face of cheaper IBM PC clones and an explosion of MS Windows software. This led directly to the deaths of SGI, Sun, Symbolics, Lucid, LMI, etc.

3. Proprietary UNIX variants like HP-UX, IRIX, AIX, and SCO Unix have basically completely died out, replaced by lower-cost proprietary OSes like Windows and MacOS, or by open-source descendants of Linux and BSD.

4. Many commercial database vendors like Oracle, dBase, Sybase, FoxPro, and Microsoft (SQL Server and Access) found themselves very much under margin pressure from PostGres, MySQL, and SQLite. Oracle survived thanks to their massive installed base and legal department, and Microsoft survived because they could cross-subsidize from their OS and Office monopolies, but dBase, Sybase, and FoxPro are no longer with us.
children.textZhHtml:
在试图回忆历史类似事件时，存在着巨大的幸存者偏差，因为在每一次利润锐减和竞争使该行业成为商品业务的情况下，大的专有名称都不再属于我们。 不过，这里是一些示例的选择：

1.内存芯片利润在 80 年代大幅下滑，以至于英特尔完全退出了内存芯片业务。 当时，他们更多地被认为是一家内存芯片公司，而不是一家微处理器公司。

2。面对更便宜的 IBM PC 克隆产品和 MS Windows 软件的爆炸式增长，高端工作站的利润大幅下降。 这直接导致了SGI、Sun、Symbolics、Lucid、LMI等的消亡。

3。 HP-UX、IRIX、AIX 和 SCO Unix 等专有 UNIX 变体基本上已经完全消失，取而代之的是 Windows 和 MacOS 等成本较低的专有操作系统，或者 Linux 和 BSD 的开源后代。

4。许多商业数据库供应商，如 Oracle、dBase、Sybase、FoxPro 和 Microsoft (SQL Server and Access) 发现自己面临来自 PostGres、MySQL 和 SQLite 的利润压力。 Oracle 幸存下来要归功于其庞大的安装基础和法律部门，而 Microsoft 幸存下来是因为他们可以从其操作系统和 Office 垄断中进行交叉补贴，但 dBase、Sybase 和 FoxPro 已不再属于我们。

children.author: cb321
children.textRawHtml: That is a good list. While I'm sure many companies could be added, I only post to include DEC https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Digital_Equipment_Corporation whose `vt50/vt100/vtXX` ideas may be with us, in software, for perpetuity unless something like Arcan (https://arcan-fe.com/) ever takes off.
children.textZhHtml: 这是一个很好的列表。 虽然我确信可以添加许多家公司，但我发帖只是为了包括 DEC https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Digital_Equipment_Corporation，其 `vt50/vt100/vtXX` 想法可能会在软件中永久存在于我们身边，除非像 Arcan (https://arcan-fe.com/) 这样的东西能够成功。

children.author: mikenew
children.textRawHtml: Arcan is such a fascinating project that I've never quite managed to get my head around.
children.textZhHtml: Arcan 是一个非常令人着迷的项目，我一直无法完全理解它。

children.author: jtolmar
children.textRawHtml:
There's a really noticeable difference in time frame covered in your examples (80s and 90s) and the one in the comment you're replying to (2010s and 2020s).

Is that just two people with different go-to examples? Or is there something going on here?

(I don't mean this as a leading question to some conclusion in my back pocket, I genuinely have no clue.)
children.textZhHtml:
您的示例中涵盖的时间范围（80 年代和 90 年代）与您回复的评论中的时间范围（2010 年代和 2020 年代）存在非常明显的差异。

这只是两个人有不同的首选示例吗？或者这里发生了什么？

（我并不是说这是一个导致我得出某些结论的引导性问题，我真的不知道。）

children.author: corford
children.textRawHtml:
It's politics.

The US's corporate problems in the 1980s and early 1990s existed when strong international competition existed.

It began to change with things like https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1986_U.S.%E2%80%93Japan_Semico... and https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plaza_Accord.

It was accelerated further with things like anti-circumvention clauses in Free Trade agreements (see Cory Doctrow's recent highlighting of this: https://pluralistic.net/2026/01/01/39c3/) and then had more gasoline thrown on the fire in the ZIRP/easy money era post GFC, culminating with the bazooka of stimulus unleashed post-covid.

My best guess is we are now going to witness ~20 years of slow unwind. You can already see signs of this in things like RoW/EM stocks outperforming the S&P, treasury yields diverging from other "safe haven" soverign bonds (e.g. swiss), gold price rising, Europe starting to get serious about addressing the Draghi report's findings, European defence spending increasing, China starting to act like the "adult in the room" wrt the recent Iran/US blow-up etc. Essentially, countries/blocs attempting to re-assert sovereignty that has been willingly diluted over the last ~30 years to mainly America's benefit.
children.textZhHtml:
这是政治。

美国在 20 世纪 80 年代和 1990 年代初存在企业问题，当时国际竞争激烈。

随着 https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1986_U.S.%E2%80%93Japan_Semico... 和 https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plaza_Accord。

自由贸易协定中的反规避条款等进一步加速了这种趋势（参见 Cory Doctrow 最近强调的这一点：https://pluralistic.net/2026/01/01/39c3/），然后在全球金融危机后的 ZIRP/宽松货币时代火上浇油，最终以刺激措施的火箭筒而达到顶峰后新冠疫情爆发。

我最好的猜测是，我们现在将见证约 20 年的缓慢放松。你已经可以在以下方面看到这种迹象：世界其他地区/新兴市场股票的表现优于标准普尔指数，国债收益率与其他“避险”主权债券（例如瑞士债券）不同，金价上涨，欧洲开始认真对待德拉吉报告的调查结果，欧洲国防支出增加，中国开始在最近的伊朗/美国冲突中表现得像“房间里的成年人”等等。重新主张在过去约 30 年里为了主要美国的利益而自愿淡化的主权。

children.author: nostrademons
children.textRawHtml:
Somewhere else in the comments here, someone else remarked "Individuals perhaps [move to the new models], but not organizations."

That's illustrative. The mechanism by which organizations are forced to update their technology, move to more competitive suppliers, and cut costs is a recession. In one, every business that doesn't do so goes bankrupt, and what's left are the more efficient businesses that have adopted technology effectively.

We haven't had a real recession since 2009. (2020 was an odd case, because it was effectively brought on by government edict and so it actually killed a number of efficient but unlucky sectors while doing nothing to clean out the dead wood in major corporations). The next one is likely to be a doozy, because the economy is filled with bullshit jobs, bullshit corporations, and bullshit products.
children.textZhHtml:
在此处评论的其他地方，其他人评论道“个人可能[转向新模型]，但不是组织。”

这很说明问题。 组织被迫更新技术、转向更有竞争力的供应商并削减成本的机制是衰退。 其一，每家不这样做的企业都会破产，剩下的是有效采用技术的效率更高的企业。

自 2009 年以来，我们还没有经历过真正的衰退。（2020 年是一个奇怪的例子，因为它是它实际上是由政府法令引发的，因此它实际上杀死了一些高效但不幸的部门，同时没有采取任何措施来清理大公司的死木）。 下一个可能会很疯狂，因为经济充满了狗屎工作、狗屎公司和狗屎产品。

children.author: Dumblydorr
children.textRawHtml:
“Brought on by government edict”

No, brought on by a novel pathogen that killed 10 million people. It would’ve been much worse without government action.
children.textZhHtml:
“由政府法令引起”

不，由一种导致 1000 万人死亡的新型病原体引起。如果没有政府采取行动，情况会更糟。

children.author: pocksuppet
children.textRawHtml:
Without the indecisive government action that was taken, millions more people would have died, but the economy would have done better.

With decisive government action (see New Zealand), millions less people would have died, and the economy would have done better.
children.textZhHtml:
如果没有采取果断的政府行动，数百万人会死亡，但经济会表现得更好。

如果政府采取果断行动（参见新西兰），死亡人数会减少数百万人，经济也会表现得更好。

children.author: klipt
children.textRawHtml:
New Zealand does have the advantage of an ocean border and thus near total control on entries and exits.

Compared to the very porous land borders of the US.
children.textZhHtml:
新西兰确实拥有海洋边界的优势，因此几乎可以完全控制出入境。

与美国漏洞百出的陆地边界相比。

children.author: pocksuppet
children.textRawHtml: Come on, they're not that porous, certainly under a Trump administration. People fly into NZ too.
children.textZhHtml: 得了吧，他们并没有那么漏洞百出，尤其是在特朗普政府的领导下。人们也飞往新西兰。

children.author: Xunjin
children.textRawHtml: This argument is so flawed in many ways, economies are built by people for people. Extrapolate the numbers, let's say in the COVID Pandemic a country, take USA as example, has 10% percentage of their population killed by it, would that be better to the economy?
children.textZhHtml: 这一论点在很多方面都存在缺陷，经济是由人民为人民而建立的。推断一下数字，假设在新冠病毒大流行中，一个国家（以美国为例）有 10% 的人口因此死亡，这对经济会更好吗？

children.author: etcimon
children.textRawHtml:
There's multiple ways to look at the economy, the raw exchange of dollar currency in a debt chase (shit I need to run faster & pay down this stuff!), there's the productivity of industrial output (shit we need to sell more junk and useless crap!), and there's the stock and productivity in optimal life cycles (Damn, that Nokia is a tank!)

If 10% of the population went away, it would affect 1 & 2, but in any true practical lens, there's a ton of cheap empty houses, while on the other hand building repairable stuff that lasts or enough cheaply is where economies move to more complex technologies by saving time and effort in useless endeavour of debt chases or consumption-oriented wasteful productivity
children.textZhHtml:
有多种方式来看待经济，债务追逐中美元货币的原始兑换（该死的我需要跑得更快并偿还这些东西！），有工业产出的生产力（该死的我们需要出售更多垃圾和无用的垃圾！），以及最佳生命周期中的库存和生产力（该死，诺基亚是一辆坦克！）

如果 10% 的人口离开，它将影响 1 & 2，但在任何真正实用的镜头中，都有大量廉价的空置房屋，而另一方面，建造持久或足够便宜的可修复的东西是经济转向更复杂的技术的地方，通过在无用的债务追逐或消费导向的浪费生产力中节省时间和精力

children.author: pocksuppet
children.textRawHtml: Economies are built by rich people for rich people. A few million poor people dead doesn't really make a dent in them.
children.textZhHtml: 经济是由富人建立并为富人服务的。几百万穷人死去，根本伤不到他们的元气。

children.author: arkh
children.textRawHtml:
> The next one is likely to be a doozy

The US, EU, China are teetering on the edge of a crisis. Russia is well on its way.

I feel like 2008 was just a warmup to what may be coming.
children.textZhHtml:
> 下一篇很可能是一场精彩的

美国、欧盟、中国都在危机的边缘摇摇欲坠。俄罗斯正在顺利前进。

我觉得 2008 年只是为即将到来的事情做准备。

children.author: davedx
children.textRawHtml: The EU is not "teetering on the edge of a crisis", why do you say that?
children.textZhHtml: 欧盟并没有“在危机的边缘摇摇欲坠”，为什么这么说？

children.author: cezart
children.textRawHtml: Not OP but, EU economy is being squeezed by China on the industrial and tech front, and by the US on the innovation/startup front. It is clear EU is no longer at the technological/economic frontier like it used to be 10-20 years ago. At the same time there are serious demographic, budgetary and political challenges all across the continent. Dragi's report covers some of these. It feels like the whole system might fall into a crisis soon if measures are not taken
children.textZhHtml: 不是OP，而是欧盟经济在工业和技术方面受到中国的挤压，在创新/创业方面受到美国的挤压。很明显，欧盟不再像 10-20 年前那样处于技术/经济前沿。与此同时，整个非洲大陆都面临着严峻的人口、预算和政治挑战。德拉吉的报告涵盖了其中一些内容。感觉如果不采取措施，整个系统很快就会陷入危机

children.author: abirch
children.textRawHtml: Factor in that Europe is powerful partly because of its unity and there are many forces trying to undermine that.
children.textZhHtml: 还要考虑到，欧洲的强大部分得益于其团结，而现在有许多势力正试图破坏这种团结。

children.author: logicchains
children.textRawHtml: Europe became powerful before it was unified, and ever since the creation of the EU it's been becoming less and less important on the world stage.
children.textZhHtml: 欧洲在统一之前就已经变得强大，而自欧盟成立以来，它在世界舞台上的地位却越来越无足轻重。

children.author: ben_w
children.textRawHtml:
We first became powerful because we did the industrial revolution before anyone else, and used more of that capacity to fight the world (and win) than to fight each other.

When we fought each other, after the industrial revolution, that was the Napoleonic Wars and the two World Wars.

> and ever since the creation of the EU it's been becoming less and less important on the world stage.

I wouldn't say it was "ever since the creation of the EU", but rather "roughly between WW1 and decolonisation". Post-Cold-War the EU has taken over from the former global importance of the member states, e.g. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brussels_effect

That said, east and South Asia are regaining their multi-millennia history of being the world's dominant power by virtue of having roughly half the total world population.

And to agree up-thread, there's plenty going that can rapidly turn the EU's economy into a disaster if not handled expertly.
children.textZhHtml:
我们最初变得强大，是因为我们比任何人都早进行了工业革命，并且将更多的产能用于对外征战（并取得了胜利），而不是用于内斗。

工业革命后我们内斗的时候，打的是拿破仑战争和两次世界大战。

> 而自欧盟成立以来，它在世界舞台上的地位却越来越无足轻重。

我不会说是“自欧盟成立以来”，而更倾向于说是“大致在一战到去殖民化之间”。冷战后，欧盟已经取代了各成员国昔日的全球地位，例如https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brussels_effect

话虽如此，东亚和南亚正凭借其约占世界总人口一半的优势，重新恢复其数千年来作为世界主导力量的历史地位。

另外，赞同楼上的观点，现在有很多事情正在发生，如果处理不当，可能会迅速让欧盟经济陷入灾难。

stories.title: Microsoft fire idTech team at Id software
stories.titleZh: 微软解雇了 Id software 的 idTech 团队

comments.author: ndiddy
comments.textRawHtml: I think we'll see stuff like this continue to happen over time. As a game company, having your own engine means that you have to be able to cultivate internal expertise in your tooling. Your employees will know this and could do bad things like ask for more money because they know that replacing them would significantly hurt productivity. Meanwhile, laying off your whole engine team and switching to UE5 means that you can get access to tons of low-wage contractors who know UE5. You can hire a bunch of them when you start a game project and then lay them all off when it's finished, and rinse and repeat as necessary. It lets you treat your employees as a replaceable commodity that can be scaled up and down as it makes monetary sense rather than a cohesive team of skilled artisans.
comments.textZhHtml: 我认为随着时间的推移，我们会看到类似的事情继续发生。作为一家游戏公司，拥有自己的引擎意味着您必须能够培养工具方面的内部专业知识。您的员工会知道这一点，并且可能会做坏事，例如索要更多钱，因为他们知道更换他们会严重损害生产力。同时，解雇整个引擎团队并切换到 UE5 意味着您可以接触到大量了解 UE5 的低薪承包商。当你开始一个游戏项目时，你可以雇佣一群人，然后在项目完成后将他们全部解雇，并根据需要进行冲洗和重复。它可以让您将您的员工视为一种可替换的商品，可以根据金钱的需要而扩大或缩小规模，而不是一个有凝聚力的熟练工匠团队。

children.author: munificent
children.textRawHtml:
All of this is true and has been true for decades in the game industry.

The other side of this seesaw is: Games are fundamentally in the novelty business. Players like some amount of familiarity, but they want new experiences. Every game engine has a sort of "grain" to it where it tends to produce games with a certain look and feel. The flat-ish shading and floaty physics of Unity is a particularly visible example of this. So using a widely used game engine can put you at a disadvantage if you're trying to make a game that doesn't go with that grain and offers players something different.

As more studios consolidate on the same engine, more players will get tired of that sameness and reward other studios more. As more studios do their own thing, players will become saturated with novelty and the benefits of not using an engine will go down. There is no stable equilibrium.
children.textZhHtml:
所有这些都是事实，并且在游戏行业几十年来一直如此。

这个跷跷板的另一面是：游戏从根本上来说是一种新奇行业。玩家喜欢一定程度的熟悉感，但他们想要新的体验。每个游戏引擎都有一种“颗粒”，它往往会产生具有特定外观和感觉的游戏。 Unity 的扁平化着色和浮动物理就是一个特别明显的例子。因此，如果您试图制作一款不符合这种要求并为玩家提供不同东西的游戏，那么使用广泛使用的游戏引擎可能会让您处于不利地位。

随着越来越多的工作室整合到同一个引擎上，越来越多的玩家会厌倦这种千篇一律的做法，并向其他工作室提供更多奖励。随着越来越多的工作室做自己的事情，玩家将被新奇事物所饱和，而不使用引擎的好处将会下降。不存在稳定平衡。

children.author: Cpoll
children.textRawHtml:
> Every game engine has a sort of "grain" to it where it tends to produce games with a certain look and feel.

I think this is a bit of a myth. Unreal gets this criticism a lot, but it's usually because many studios choose to stick close to the rendering defaults, which does lead to a certain look.

To that point, it's probably a lot cheaper to configure Unreal or Unity into a unique "grain" than it is to develop your own engine. It's also possible to use custom physics instead of those built into the engine.
children.textZhHtml:
> 每个游戏引擎都有一种“颗粒”，它往往会产生具有特定外观和感觉的游戏。

我认为这有点神话。虚幻经常受到这种批评，但这通常是因为许多工作室选择坚持渲染默认值，这确实导致了某种外观。

就这一点而言，将虚幻或 Unity 配置为独特的“颗粒”可能比开发自己的引擎便宜得多。还可以使用自定义物理场而不是引擎中内置的物理场。

children.author: willis936

children.author: midnightclubbed
children.textRawHtml:
id tech is probably the last engine that used a signature game to showcase the engine to licensees. The business model of idTech, Unreal Engine, and Crytech used to be 'make amazing looking game that sells the engine to licensees'. The problem with that model was you got a cut of the game code for whatever the 'amazing looking' game was and told to figure it out. If you wanted to make a Gears of War clone then Unreal Engine 3 was perfect... for every other game genre you had to rewrite and implement huge chunks of code.

Unreal Engine managed to evolve and expand the engine to the point where it is somewhat game agnostic, crytech became lumberyard under Amazon and died, idTech became the engine solely for iD games (ie the same business model as every other game or publisher custom engine).

iD tech's rendering pipeline is focussed on the one AA game iD are publishing that year and making that one game run efficiently and beautifully on mid-high end PC and current consoles. UE5 is focussed on providing tools for anyone to (relatively) easily make good looking games and applications that can be published to a wide range of devices.
children.textZhHtml:
id tech 可能是最后一个使用签名游戏向授权商展示引擎的引擎。 idTech、Unreal Engine 和 Crytech 的商业模式曾经是“制作外观精美的游戏，然后将引擎出售给授权商”。 该模型的问题在于，无论“外观令人惊叹”的游戏是什么，您都会获得游戏代码的一部分，并被告知要弄清楚它。 如果你想制作《战争机器》的克隆版，那么虚幻引擎 3 是完美的...对于你必须重写和实现大量代码的其他游戏类型。

虚幻引擎成功地发展和扩展了引擎，使其在某种程度上与游戏无关，crytech 成为亚马逊旗下的木材场并消亡，idTech 成为专门用于 iD 游戏的引擎（即与所有其他游戏或发行商自定义引擎相同的商业模式）。

iD tech 的渲染管道是专注于 iD 当年发布的一款 AA 游戏，并让这款游戏在中高端 PC 和当前游戏机上高效、美观地运行。 UE5 专注于为任何人提供工具（相对）轻松地制作可发布到各种设备的精美游戏和应用程序。

children.textZhHtml: 是的，这是基本率谬误情况的完美示例...即使扫描仪的准确率达到 99.99%，因为更高比例的照片是无辜的，扫描仪发现的大多数匹配都将是误报。

children.author: wesammikhail
children.textRawHtml:
Funny you bring this up.

Back in the day when I was like 15 and DC++ was still a thing, I used to browse people's shared folders. One day I came across a file called "the paradox of false positive". It was a 1 pager that described how a machine which is 99.9% accurate at identifying terrorists would be completely useless due to this false positive base rate fallacy you're describing.

It really stuck with me throughout the years. It's kind o remarkable how even a 99.9% accurate heuristic is insufficient at scale.

Which begs the question: lets assume the intentions are pure (which we know they're not but lets be generous), what other options are there when 99.9% heuristic is not good enough? how do you design systems when they're guaranteed to fail as they scale up?

edit: and what do you know, I just saw this as I scrolled down on HN https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48816959
children.textZhHtml:
有趣的是你提到了这个。

当我 15 岁的时候，DC++ 还很流行，我曾经浏览人们的共享文件夹。有一天，我看到一份名为“误报悖论”的文件。这是一个 1 寻呼机，它描述了一台识别恐怖分子准确率达到 99.9% 的机器如何由于您所描述的误报基本率谬误而变得完全无用。

多年来，它一直困扰着我。值得注意的是，即使是 99.9% 准确的启发式方法在规模上也是不够的。

这就引出了一个问题：让我们假设意图是纯粹的（我们知道它们不是，但让我们慷慨一些），当 99.9% 启发式方法不够好时，还有哪些其他选择？当系统规模扩大时注定会失败时，你如何设计系统？

children.author: DiggyJohnson

I genuinely think it’s all three:

1. The cultural factor is rising expectations for children and their parents, costing both time and money;

2. The political/social factor is nanny states and academic institutions that the public expects to not only teach but raise their kids;

3. Technology. Especially the Internet, mobile devices, social media, and short form content. Technology distracts and isolates both kids and parents.

An example of the three factors at work is the all-too-common local news trope of ”Nosy neighbor calls CPS because the family next door lets their kids walk to school. Whole family traumatized as a result.”
children.textZhHtml:
我真的认为这三个都是：

1.文化因素是对孩子及其父母的期望不断提高，这会花费时间和金钱；

2。政治/社会因素是保姆国家和学术机构，公众不仅期望它们教育孩子，还期望它们抚养孩子；

3。技术。尤其是互联网、移动设备、社交媒体和简短内容。技术会分散孩子和家长的注意力并使其孤立。

这三个因素的一个例子是当地非常常见的新闻比喻：“爱管闲事的邻居打电话给 CPS，因为隔壁的家庭让他们的孩子步行上学。结果整个家庭都受到了创伤。”

stories.title: It's not about physical vs. digital games, it's about ownership
stories.titleZh: 这无关实体游戏与数字游戏之分，关键在于所有权。

comments.author: jbombadil
comments.textRawHtml:
I am generally not in favor of adding regulation, but this is a place where I would support it.

Anything that you BUY needs to be your property. This means you must have the ability to:

1. Transfer ownership of it (either temporarily as a loan or permanently as a sale). Digital-only doesn't preclude this: the store can have a "transfer" functionality.

2. (Within reason) use it at your discretion at any point after the sale. This means that a company cannot "revoke" your access at a later time. Specifically for content that is DRM locked, if they decide to sunset that service (store, DRM server, whatever), no problem! just offer DRM free (or generally lock-free copies). I have no problem with Sony not offering DRM free versions of games that I can still download and play with the store. But if that goes away -> you must give me a path to local ownership.

(Multiplayer games that require server infrastructure are a bit more complex, and I'd leave aside for now).

This should apply equally to video games, movies, books, music. Any digital content.
comments.textZhHtml:
我一般不赞成增加监管，但这是我支持的地方。

您购买的任何东西都必须是您的财产。这意味着您必须具备以下能力：

1.转让其所有权（暂时作为贷款或永久作为出售）。纯数字并不排除这一点：商店可以具有“转移”功能。

2. （在合理范围内）销售后您可以随时自行决定使用它。这意味着公司以后无法“撤销”您的访问权限。特别是对于 DRM 锁定的内容，如果他们决定取消该服务（商店、DRM 服务器等），没问题！只需提供免费的 DRM（或通常无锁的副本）。我对索尼不提供 DRM 免费版本的游戏没有问题，我仍然可以在商店下载和玩这些游戏。但如果这种情况消失了 -> 你必须给我一条获得本地所有权的途径。

（需要服务器基础设施的多人游戏有点复杂，我现在先把它放在一边）。

这应该同样适用于视频游戏、电影、书籍、音乐。任何数字内容。
